full_notes:
Water in the Atmosphere is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that w W W W WORLD ORLD ORLD ORLD ORLD C C C C CLIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE AND AND AND AND AND C C C C CLIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE C C C C CHANGE HANGE HANGE HANGE HANGE T he world climate can be studied by organising information and data on climate and synthesising them in smaller units for easy understanding, description and analysis.

The text explains that three broad approaches have been adopted for classifying climate.

The text explains that empirical classification is based on observed data, particularly on temperature and precipitation.

The text explains that genetic classification attempts to organise climates according to their causes.

The text explains that applied classification is for specific purpose.

The text explains that k K K K K O E P P O E P P O E P P O E P P O E P P E N E N E N E N E N ’’’’’ SSSSS S S S S SC H E M E C H E M E C H E M E C H E M E C H E M E O F O F O F O F O F C C C C CL A S S I F I C A T I O N L A S S I F I C A T I O N L A S S I F I C A T I O N L A S S I F I C A T I O N L A S S I F I C A T I O N OF OF OF OF OF C C C C CLIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE LIMATE The most widely used classification of climate is the empirical climate classification scheme developed by V.

The text explains that koeppen identified a close relationship between the distribution of vegetation and climate.

The text explains that he selected certain values of temperature and precipitation and related them to the distribution of vegetation and used these values for classifying the climates.

The text explains that it is an empirical classification based on mean annual and mean monthly temperature and precipitation data.

The text explains that he introduced the use of capital and small letters to designate climatic groups and types.

The text explains that although developed in 1918 and modified over a period of time, Koeppen’s scheme is still popular and in use.

The text explains that koeppen recognised five major climatic groups, four of them are based on temperature and one on precipitation.

The text explains that the capital letters : A,C, D and E delineate humid climates and B dry climates.

The text explains that the climatic groups are subdivided into types, designated by small letters, based on seasonality of precipitation and temperature characteristics.

The text explains that the seasons of dryness are indicated by the small letters : f, m, w and s, where f corresponds to no dry season, Table 11.1 : Climatic Groups According to Koeppen Table 11.1 : Climatic Groups According to Koeppen Table 11.1 : Climatic Groups According to Koeppen Table 11.1 : Climatic Groups According to Koeppen Table 11.1 : Climatic Groups According to Koeppen Group Characteristics A - Tropical Average temperature of the coldest month is 18° C or higher B - Dry Climates Potential evaporation exceeds precipitation C - Warm Temperate The average temperature of the coldest month of the (Mid-latitude) climates years is higher than minus 3°C but below 18°C D - Cold Snow Forest Climates The average temperature of the coldest month is minus 3° C or below E - Cold Climates Average temperature for all months is below 10° C C H A P T E R

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 92 m - monsoon climate, w- winter dry season and s - summer dry season.

The text explains that the small letters a, b, c and d refer to the degree of severity of temperature.

The text explains that annual range of temperature is very low and annual rainfall is high.

The text explains that the temperature is uniformly high and the annual range of temperature is negligible.

The text explains that the maximum temperature on any day is around 30°C while the minimum temperature is around 20°C.

The text explains that temperature is high throughout the year and diurnal ranges of temperature are the greatest in the dry season.

The text explains that at low latitudes, from 15° - 30°, they occur in the area of subtropical high where subsidence and inversion of temperature do not produce rainfall.

The text explains that in middle latitudes, from 35° - 60° north and south of equator, they are confined to the interior of continents where maritime-humid winds do not reach and to areas often surrounded by mountains.

The text explains that subtropical Steppe (BSh) and Subtropical Desert (BWh) Climates Subtropical steppe (BSh) and subtropical desert (BWh) have common precipitation and temperature characteristics.

The text explains that maximum temperature in the summer is very high.

The text explains that the highest shade temperature of 58° C was recorded at Al Aziziyah, Libya on 13 September 1922.

The text explains that the annual and diurnal ranges of temperature are also high.

The text explains that humid Subtropical Climate (Cwa) Humid subtropical climate occurs poleward of Tropic of Cancer and Capricorn, mainly in North Indian plains and South China interior plains.

The text explains that the climate is similar to Aw climate except that the temperature in winter is warm.

The text explains that monthly average temperature in summer is around 25° C and in winter below 10°C.

The text explains that mean monthly temperature in summer is around 27°C, and in winter it varies from 5°-12° C.

The text explains that due to marine influence, the temperature is moderate and in winter, it is warmer than for its latitude.

The text explains that the mean temperature in summer months ranges from 15°-20°C and in winter 4°-10°C.

The text explains that the annual and daily ranges of temperature are small.

The text explains that poleward summer temperatures are lower and winter temperatures are extremely low with many locations experiencing below freezing point temperatures for up to seven months in a year.

The text explains that ice Cap Climate (EF) The ice cap climate (EF) occurs over interior Greenland and Antartica.

The text explains that even in summer, the temperature is below freezing point.

The text explains that the snow and ice get accumulated and the mounting pressure causes the deformation of the ice sheets and they break.

The text explains that wORLD CLIMATE AND CLIMATE CHANGE 95 C C C C CLIM LIM LIM LIM LIMATE ATE ATE ATE ATE C C C C CHANGE HANGE HANGE HANGE HANGE The earlier chapters on climate summarised our understanding of climate as it prevails now.

The text explains that the planet earth has witnessed many variations in climate since the beginning.

The text explains that the geomorphological features, especially in high altitudes and high latitudes, exhibit traces of advances and retreats of glaciers.

The text explains that all these evidences indicate that change in climate is a natural and continuous process.

The text explains that in the geological past, the earth was warm some 500-300 million years ago, through the Cambrian, Ordovician and Silurian periods.

The text explains that the 1990s recorded the warmest temperature of the century and some of the worst floods around the world.

The text explains that from about 1885-1940 world temperature showed an upward trend.

The text explains that after 1940, the rate of increase in temperature slowed down.

The text explains that (a) High rainfall in all the months (b) Mean monthly temperature of the coldest month more than freezing point (c) Mean monthly temperature of all the months more than 18o C (d) Average temperature for all the months below 10° C (ii) Koeppen’s system of classification of climates can be termed as : (a) Applied (b) Systematic (c) Genetic (d) Empirical (iii) Most of the Indian Peninsula will be grouped according to Koeppen’s system under: (a) “Af” (b) “BSh” (c) “Cfb” (d) “Am” (iv) Which one of the following years is supposed to have recorded the warmest temperature the world over?

The text explains that (a) 1990 (b) 1998 (c) 1885 (d) 1950 (v) Which one of the following groups of four climates represents humid conditions?

The text explains that (a) A—B—C—E (b) A—C—D—E (c) B—C—D—E (d) A—C—D—F 2.

The text explains that (i) Which two climatic variables are used by Koeppen for classification of the climate?

The text explains that (ii) How is the “genetic” system of classification different from the “empirical one”?

The text explains that (iii) Which types of climates have very low range of temperature?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 11: Water in the Atmosphere
